coredata.dc:title: Zwitterionic nanogels crosslinked by fluorescent carbon dots for targeted drug delivery and simultaneous bioimaging
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2016 Acta Materialia Inc. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Acta Materialia Inc. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Wenchen
Liu, Qingsheng
Zhang, Peng
Liu, Lingyun
coredata.dc:description:
A zwitterionic multifunctional nanogel drug delivery vehicle was synthesized by copolymerizing ornithine methacrylamide (OrnAA, a newly developed amino acid – derived zwitterionic non-fouling monomer) with fluorescent crosslinkable carbon dots (CCDs). In this construct, the zwitterionic nanogel network served as a functionalizable non-fouling matrix for drug loading, while the introduction of CCDs as crosslinkers enabled the real-time tracking and locating of the nanogel. The nanogels showed exceptional stability when incubated in protein solutions and stable fluorescence similar to that of CCDs. Labeled dextran was encapsulated in nanogels as a model drug, and was released in a controlled manner. Importantly, cellular uptake experiments showed that the folic acid – conjugated nanogels can be specifically internalized by the folate receptor – overexpressed cancer cells, but not in normal tissue cells. This type of multifunctional nanogels holds great potential for targeted delivery and simultaneous imaging in cancer therapy.
Statement of Significance In this work, we developed a zwitterionic multifunctional nanogel drug delivery system, by copolymerizing ornithine methacrylamide (OrnAA, a newly developed amino acid – derived zwitterionic non-fouling monomer) with fluorescent crosslinkable carbon dots (CCDs). The non-fouling pOrnAA network provides the nanogels with great stability in biophysical environments and serves as a matrix for drug loading, whereas the fluorescent CCDs not only serve as crosslinkers but also provide stable (i.e., non-photobleaching) fluorescence signals for real-time tracking of the nanogels in the delivery process. A model drug dextran loaded in the nanogels was shown to be released in a controlled manner. Furthermore, the abundant functional groups possessed by pOrnAA can be further conjugated with ligands for specific cell targeting. Our results show that folic acid-modified nanogels were only selectively internalized in folate receptor overexpressed cancer cells, but not in normal tissue cells. Such multifunctional zwitterionic nanogels hold great potential for targeted drug delivery and simultaneous imaging in cancer therapy, due to their great stability, bioimaging capability, excellent biocompatibility, controlled drug release, and selective cell targeting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Zwitterionic
Multifunctional nanogels
Poly(ornithine methacrylamide)
Carbon dots
Folic acid
Targeted delivery

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Release of the encapsulated dextran from nanogels
In vitro cellular uptake and confocal microscopy

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2016 Acta Materialia Inc. Published by Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) PL emission spectra of p(CD-OrnAA) nanogels with different weight percentages of CCD crosslinkers at 340nm excitation. (b) UV–Vis spectra of CCDs, FA, p(CD-OrnAA), and FA-p(CD-OrnAA). (c) Photos of p(CD-OrnAA) solution under visible (left) and 365-nm UV (right) light. PL emission spectra of (d) p(CD-OrnAA) (the insert is the normalized PL emission spectra) and (e) FA-p(CD-OrnAA).
Stability of p(CD-OrnAA) and FA-p(CD-OrnAA) nanogels in (a) 5wt% BSA and (b) 5wt% Fg solution in PBS for 24h.
FITC-dextran release from p(CD-OrnAA) nanogels. The results were averaged from three replicates.
Viability of NIH/3T3 fibroblasts after treated with CCDs, p(CD-OrnAA), and FA-p(CD-OrnAA) for 24h.

Laser scanning confocal microscopy images of SKOV3 cells incubated for 12h with p(CD-OrnAA), FA-p(CD-OrnAA), or FA-p(CD-OrnAA) with cells pretreated with excessive folic acid. Control samples were not treated with any substance. Scale bar=50μm. Fluorescence signals of Hoechst 33342 (blue, for cell nuclei) and CCDs (green) were observed under excitations at 405 and 488nm, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Sequential z-stack images of SKOV3 cells treated with FA-p(CD-OrnAA) (Bottom: 0μm; Top: 10.36μm). Scale bar=50μm. Fluorescence signals of Hoechst 33342 (blue, for cell nuclei) and CCDs (green) were observed under excitations at 405 and 488nm, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Enlarged image of SKOV3 cells treated with FA-p(CD-OrnAA) at z=4.44μm. Scale bar=50μm.

Laser scanning confocal microscopy images of NIH/3T3 cells incubated for 12h with p(CD-OrnAA) or FA-p(CD-OrnAA) nanogels. Control samples were not treated with any substance. Scale bar=50μm. Fluorescence signals of Hoechst 33342 (blue, for nuclei) and CCDs (green) were observed under excitations at 405 and 488nm, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Sizes (average±standard deviation, n=4) of p(CD-OrnAA) nanogels in PBS measured by DLS. The weight percentage of CCDs represents the weight ratio in feed.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: Part of the Special Issue on Zwitterionic Materials, organized by Professors Shaoyi Jiang, Kazuhiko Ishihara, and Jian Ji.

head.ce:title: Zwitterionic nanogels crosslinked by fluorescent carbon dots for targeted drug delivery and simultaneous bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering, University of Akron, Akron, OH 44325, United States

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biomolecular Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, University of Washington, Seattle, WA 98195, United States

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A zwitterionic multifunctional nanogel drug delivery vehicle was synthesized by copolymerizing ornithine methacrylamide (OrnAA, a newly developed amino acid – derived zwitterionic non-fouling monomer) with fluorescent crosslinkable carbon dots (CCDs). In this construct, the zwitterionic nanogel network served as a functionalizable non-fouling matrix for drug loading, while the introduction of CCDs as crosslinkers enabled the real-time tracking and locating of the nanogel. The nanogels showed exceptional stability when incubated in protein solutions and stable fluorescence similar to that of CCDs. Labeled dextran was encapsulated in nanogels as a model drug, and was released in a controlled manner. Importantly, cellular uptake experiments showed that the folic acid – conjugated nanogels can be specifically internalized by the folate receptor – overexpressed cancer cells, but not in normal tissue cells. This type of multifunctional nanogels holds great potential for targeted delivery and simultaneous imaging in cancer therapy.

In this work, we developed a zwitterionic multifunctional nanogel drug delivery system, by copolymerizing ornithine methacrylamide (OrnAA, a newly developed amino acid – derived zwitterionic non-fouling monomer) with fluorescent crosslinkable carbon dots (CCDs).

The non-fouling pOrnAA network provides the nanogels with great stability in biophysical environments and serves as a matrix for drug loading, whereas the fluorescent CCDs not only serve as crosslinkers but also provide stable (i.e., non-photobleaching) fluorescence signals for real-time tracking of the nanogels in the delivery process. A model drug dextran loaded in the nanogels was shown to be released in a controlled manner. Furthermore, the abundant functional groups possessed by pOrnAA can be further conjugated with ligands for specific cell targeting. Our results show that folic acid-modified nanogels were only selectively internalized in folate receptor overexpressed cancer cells, but not in normal tissue cells.

Such multifunctional zwitterionic nanogels hold great potential for targeted drug delivery and simultaneous imaging in cancer therapy, due to their great stability, bioimaging capability, excellent biocompatibility, controlled drug release, and selective cell targeting.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanocarriers with intrinsic fluorescent properties have drawn great attention as multifunctional drug delivery systems for real-time tracking or simultaneous diagnosis and therapy [1,2]. A successful design of such nanocarriers should provide several key features including non-fouling surfaces which resist nonspecific protein adsorption, amenability to be functionalized for specific targeting or diagnostic purpose, efficient drug releasing, proper particle size to prolong in vivo circulation, good biocompatibility, and ability to monitor the particles with both spatial and temporal resolutions [3–7]. Though numerous particles have been studied for this application, few delivery systems can fulfill all the above stated criteria.

Non-fouling surface is a key feature for long circulating nanoparticles [6,8]. Zwitterionic polymers bearing both positively and negatively charged groups equimolarly in one moiety have been recognized as non-fouling materials and widely used to improve nanoparticle stability and in vivo circulation half-life [9,10]. Among various zwitterionic molecules, amino acid based zwitterionic polymers (pAAZ) are of particular interest due to their ultra-low fouling and functionalizable properties. In our previous work [11–14], several amino acid derived polymers including poly(serine methacrylate) (pSerMA), poly(lysine methacrylamide) (pLysAA), poly(ornithine methacrylamide) (pOrnAA), poly(N4-(2-methacrylamidoethyl) asparagine) (pAspAA) and poly(N5-(2-methacrylamidoethyl) glutamine) (pGluAA) have been proved ultra-low fouling, strongly resisting adhesion from protein, cell, and bacteria. Meanwhile, both amino and carboxyl terminal groups allow facile biomolecular conjugation for additional functions. These properties render pAAZ an ideal class of materials to construct multifunctional drug delivery nanoparticles. Because of the ease preparation and best protein resistance performance of pOrnAA among others, it was chosen to construct the nanosized hydrogels for multifunctional drug delivery vehicles in this study, to achieve controlled drug release in the long circulation particle size window (20–200nm) [15].

Various fluorescent structures, such as organic dyes [16], quantum dots [17], and carbon dots (CDs) [18,19], have been used to introduce fluorescence into drug delivery vehicles. CDs exhibit great advantages over organic dyes and quantum dots due to their exceptional photostability (i.e., non-photobleaching), ease in preparation, and excellent biocompatibility [20,21]. However, how to incorporate CDs into zwitterionic nanogel vehicles remains a challenge. Direct conjugation of CDs onto nanogel surfaces will sacrifice non-fouling property of the nanogels; whereas physical encapsulation will result in decreased drug loading capacity and undesired carbon dot leaking. To avoid these detrimental effects, here we developed a novel approach which utilizes crosslinkable carbon dots (CCDs) as crosslinkers in nanogel preparation, incorporating fluorescence into nanogels without compromising any desirable characteristics.

In this work, a multifunctional drug delivery system fulfilling all the above-stated criteria has been constructed. The pOrnAA network of nanogels provides non-fouling functionalizable matrix for drug loading; while CCDs serve dual functions as both crosslinkers and imaging agent. Stability of such constructs in protein solutions and their photoluminescence emission properties were investigated. Furthermore, folic acid (FA) was conjugated onto the nanogel surfaces as a targeting ligand. Fluorescently labeled dextran was used as a model drug to study the drug loading and releasing characteristics. Finally, cellular uptake was examined to determine if the FA - conjugated nanocarriers can be specifically internalized by the cancer cells overexpressing folate receptors.

Acrylic acid (99%), 1, 2-ethylenediamine (98%), glycidyl methacrylate (97%), l-ornithine hydrochloride (99%), methacryloyl chloride (97%), 8-hydroxyquinoline, folic acid, and N–hydroxysulfosuccinimide (NHS) were obtained from Alfa Aesar (Ward Hill, MA). Copper(II) carbonate basic, fluorescein isothiocyanate-dextran (FITC-dextran; MW 10000), Tween 80, Span 80, bovine serum albumin (BSA), and phosphate-buffered saline (PBS; pH 7.4, 10mM, 138mM NaCl, 2.7mM KCl) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Milwaukee, WI). 2,2′-Azobis(4-methoxy-2,4-dimethyl valeronitrile) (V-70) was purchased from Wako Pure Chemical Industries (Osaka, Japan). 1-Ethyl-3-[3-dimethylaminopropyl]-carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC) was purchased from Pierce Biotechnology (Rockford, IL). Human plasma fibrinogen (Fg) was obtained from EMD Biosciences (San Diego, CA). 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) and Hoechst 33342 nucleic acid stain were purchased from Life Technologies (Carlsbad, CA). Dulbecco’s modified Eagle’s medium (DMEM; with 4.5g/L glucose, 4mM l-glutamine and 110mg/L sodium pyruvate) was obtained from Thermo Scientific (Waltham, MA). Nonessential amino acids and sodium pyruvate were purchased from Lonza (Walkersville, MD). Penicillin streptomycin was purchased from Invitrogen (Grand Island, NY). N-δ-methacryloyl ornithine (i.e. ornithine methacrylamide, OrnAA) was synthesized by the reaction of l-ornithine hydrochloride with methacryloyl chloride using the method published before [13].

CCDs were prepared according to a previous published work [22]. Briefly, 5.49ml acrylic acid (80mmol) and 5.35ml ethylenediamine (80mmol) were dissolved in 40ml water. After vigorous stirring, the solution-containing beaker was put in a 700W domestic microwave oven. The reaction was stopped when the solution turned red-brown with a light smoke appearing at about 7min, followed by adding 20ml DI water to dissolve the formed carbon dots. 1H NMR spectrum (data not shown) of the produced carbon dots showed no obvious peaks from acrylic acid and ethylenediamine, indicating the reactants had reacted or evaporated during the process. To prepare CCDs, 10ml glycidyl methacrylate was added to the above carbon dot solution. After stirring for 24h at room temperature, water phase was separated and washed by hexane. The unreacted glycidyl methacrylate was washed away by hexane and no residue was seen through thin layer chromatography. The final solution was lyophilized to obtain dry CCDs (9.6g; yield: ∼90%).

P(CD-OrnAA) nanogels were prepared using OrnAA as the monomer and CCD as the crosslinker, by inverse microemulsion free radical polymerization. In the continuous phase, 1.4g of Tween 80, 1.6g of Span 80, and 8mg of V-70 initiator were dissolved well in 40ml hexane. 0.3g OrnAA and 15mg CCDs were dissolved in 1.5ml PBS buffer as the dispersed phase. Under vigorous mixing, the dispersed phase was added to the continuous phase with further sonication for 2min. The mixed solution was kept in ice bath and purged with nitrogen for 30min. The reaction was kept at 40°C for 4h under nitrogen atmosphere.

To purify nanogels, 120ml tetrahydrofuran (THF) was added into 40ml reaction solution and stirred for 4h to remove surfactant. The mixed solution was then centrifuged at 6000rpm for 20min. After discarding supernatant, the precipitate was further washed twice by THF. The final precipitate was suspended in 4ml water and transferred into a 100kD molecular weight cutoff Amicon Ultra centrifugal filter device (Millipore, Burlington, MA) to remove impurities and water. This purification process was repeated five times. To ensure purity of the final product, filtrate was scanned under UV–vis spectrometer to examine if there was characteristic adsorption from residual monomer or CCDs. Finally, the nanogel solution was filtered through a sterile 0.45μm PTFE syringe filter and lyophilized for further use. 140.2mg p(CD-OrnAA) was obtained (yield: 44%). To encapsulate FITC-dextran in nanogels, the preparation procedure was the same as that for p(CD-OrnAA), except for the addition of 10mg FITC-dextran in the water phase before the microemulsion polymerization.

Folic acid (FA) was firstly activated by the EDC/NHS chemistry. Specifically, 4mg FA was dissolved in a water/dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) mixture (4ml, 3:2). 2.4mg EDC and 1.4mg NHS were then added into the FA solution. The activation was kept for 2h at room temperature. The solution of the activated FA was then added dropwise into 5ml aqueous solution of p(CD-OrnAA) nanogels (20mg), and incubated overnight at room temperature. After reaction, the solution pH was adjusted to 9–10, followed by an ultra-centrifuge purification step to remove the unreacted FA and other reactants. The purification step was repeated three times, followed by washing with DI water five times. To ensure purity of the final product, filtrate was scanned under UV–vis spectrometer to examine if there was characteristic adsorption from residual FA. The solution was finally freeze-dried to get FA-p(CD-OrnAA) (17.8mg, yield: ∼88%).

The size of crosslinkable carbon dots was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL, JSM-1230). UV–Vis absorption was measured on a UV-1601 Shimadzu spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) emission measurements were performed on a Hitachi F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The normalized spectrum was obtained by dividing the absolute intensity of the PL spectrum by the maximum value of its own. The hydrodynamic diameters of p(CD-OrnAA) and FA-p(CD-OrnAA) nanogels were characterized on a Zetasizer NanoZS dynamic light scattering (DLS) instrument (Malvern, U.K.) at a wavelength of 633nm and a scattering angle of 173°. The dispersant refractive index and the viscosity of PBS were taken as 1.330 and 0.8872 cP, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Release of the encapsulated dextran from nanogels
ce:section.ce:para: 40mg FITC-dextran encapsulated p(CD-OrAA) nanogels was dissolved in 20ml water. Initially, the total fluorescence intensity of the nanogel solution was taken. Over the next 3weeks, at different days, 1ml nanogel solution was taken out and centrifuged using an Amicon Ultra centrifugal filter device (100 kD cutoff) at 10,000rpm for 30min. The fluorescence intensity of the flow through was then recorded with a Hitachi F-4500 fluorescence spectrophotometer at an excitation wavelength of 495nm and a cutoff wavelength of 500nm. Percentage of the released FITC-dextran was determined from the ratio of the flow-through fluorescence intensity measured at different time points to the total fluorescence intensity.

Cytotoxicity of CCDs, p(CD-OrnAA) nanogels and FA-p(CD-OrnAA) nanogels was assessed against NIH/3T3 fibroblasts through the MTT assay. NIH/3T3 cells were seeded in a 96-well culture plate at 104 cells/well 24h prior to the test. The culture medium, consisting of DMEM, 10% fetal bovine serum, 1% nonessential amino acids, 1% sodium pyruvate, and 2% penicillin streptomycin, was then refreshed and different particles at various concentrations (0.1, 0.5, 1.0, or 2.5mg/ml) were added. After another 24h culture at 37°C, the culture medium was replaced with 100μl of phenol red-free medium containing 10μl MTT solution (12mM in PBS), and incubated for another 4h. The medium was then removed. 150μl DMSO was added into each well, followed by 10-min incubation and 15-min shaking. The absorbance of each well was finally read at 570nm using a 96-well plate reader (SpectraMax M5, Molecular Devices). Each measurement had six replicates. Non-treated cells were used as control and the cell viability percentage was defined as the ratio of absorbance from the particle-treated wells to that from control. Statistical analysis was performed using Student’s t-test, defining p<0.05 as significant difference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro cellular uptake and confocal microscopy
ce:section.ce:para: SKOV3 cells (ATCC, St. Cloud, MN) and NIH/3T3 fibroblasts were separately seeded on a Nalgene Lab-Tek II eight-well chamber slide (Waltham, MA) at 2×104 cells/well 24h before use. The medium in each well was then refreshed with 2.5mg/ml p(CD-OrnAA) or FA-p(CD-OrnAA) nanogel solution in 200μl fresh culture medium. After 12h incubation, the medium was removed and each well was washed with PBS three times. Then the cells were fixed with 4wt% paraformaldehyde in PBS for 30min, and washed with PBS for three times. Before observation, 100μL of Hoechst 33342 (2μg/ml) was incubated with cells for 30min to stain the cell nuclei. The slide was then observed under an Olympus Fluoview FV1000 confocal laser scanning microscopy with excitations at 405 and 488nm for Hoechst 33342 and carbon dots, respectively. For the FA receptor inhibition assay, similar experimental procedure was utilized, except that 100-fold molar excess of FA was added to the wells 2h before adding the FA-p(CD-OrnAA) nanogels.

CCDs with high quantum yield were synthesized following a published method [22]. Multifunctional nanogel particles, denoted p(CD-OrnAA), were prepared via inverse microemulsion free radical polymerization using OrnAA as monomer and CCDs as crosslinker, as shown in Scheme 1 . The obtained nanogels were then conjugated with folic acid through standard EDC/NHS coupling chemistry to introduce specific targeting ligands. The finally obtained nanocarrier was denoted as FA-p(CD-OrnAA). Fig. S1 (supporting information) shows CCDs had a relatively uniform size distribution with the average size around 5.7nm. Previous work reported that CCDs prepared with the same method are amorphous rather than graphitic, shown by X-ray diffraction [22]. CCDs exhibited multicolor fluorescent emission depending on different excitations, as the PL spectra presented in Fig. S2. When the excitation wavelength changed from 340 to 460nm, they exhibited an excitation dependent emission behavior, which was consistent with previous work [22].

Particle size is one of the most important parameters affecting the in vivo circulation time of nanocarriers. It is generally accepted that 20–200nm is the optimal size window for drug delivery nanoparticles. If the nanoparticles are too large (>200nm), they will be easily removed by liver and splenic filtration, whereas the small nanoparticles (<20nm) can be quickly excreted through renal clearance. In some studies, it was suggested that the nanoparticles around 120nm can avoid being trapped in the hepatic parenchymal space [3]. To optimize the nanoparticle size, p(CD-OrnAA) nanogels with different crosslinking densities were synthesized. The hydrodynamic size of nanogels can be tuned from 114.2 to 170.8nm by varying the CCD percentage from 10% to 0.5%, as shown in Table 1 . After the inverse microemulsion polymerization, the size of nanogels in PBS is mainly controlled by their swelling property. More crosslinker limited the movement of polymer chains, resulting in less swelling in PBS thus smaller hydrodynamic size. Similar phenomenon has also been reported by other researchers [23]. Although more carbon dots take more space, considering the highly porous polymer gel network and the small percentage of carbon dots, their space occupancy effect on the nanogel size can be negligible. Since CCDs serve both as crosslinkers and fluorescent tags, PL performance of nanogels with varying crosslinking densities needs to be compared. As shown in Fig. 1 a, the PL intensity increases with the crosslinking density. The non-linear increase of the integrated fluorescence intensity with the feed percentage of CCDs (shown in Fig. S3) may be attributed to the incomplete conversion of CCD crosslinkers during emulsion polymerization. The possible self-quenching effect when the local CCD concentration inside a nanogel gets higher may be another reason. The following discussion will be based on p(CD-OrnAA) with 10% crosslinkers because of its optimal particle size and PL intensity.

P(CD-OrnAA) and FA-p(CD-OrnAA) were further characterized using UV–Vis in comparison with CCDs and FA. As seen in Fig. 1b, the absorption shoulder of p(CD-OrnAA) appearing around 270–350nm coincided with the characteristic shoulder position of CCDs. Furthermore, the optical absorbance curve of FA-p(CD-OrnAA) showed the characteristics peak of FA (at 283nm) [24], indicating successful fabrication of the functionalized nanogels. Another key evidence showing the successful FA conjugation is the change of surface charge. Since the conjugation process consumed a small portion of the surface amine groups of p(CD-OrnAA), the nanogel zeta-potential changed from −8.27±0.71mV to −10.35±0.34mV after FA conjugation. The extent of FA conjugation was determined by UV–Vis absorption spectrophotometry by comparing the intensity of the characteristic peak at 365nm to the standard curves of FA and CCDs. The weight percentage of FA conjugated on FA-p(CD-OrnAA) was calculated as 0.98wt% (details of how to determine the extent of FA conjugation were described in the Supporting Information). Upon irradiation by UV light (365nm), the FA-p(CD-OrnAA) aqueous solution showed strong blue emission (Fig. 1c). Similar to CCDs, the emission peaks of p(CD-OrnAA) was also dependent upon the excitation wavelength (Fig. 1d). The PL spectra of FA-p(CD-OrnAA) (Fig. 1e) was identical to those from its unmodified precursor and CCDs, showing the well documented wavelength-dependent PL behavior [25]. All these evidences indicate that the optical characteristics of CCDs were neither affected by nanogel preparation nor FA conjugation.

As stated above, surface non-fouling property is crucial for drug delivery nanoparticles when considering in vivo applications. When intravenously injected, the major challenge for nanoparticles is the adsorption of various blood proteins. These adsorbed proteins not only destabilize nanoparticles but also tag them as “foreign” thus facilitating their fast clearance by immune cells [26]. Reducing protein adsorption on nanoparticle surfaces using non-fouling material coatings has been recognized as an efficient way to extend the systemic circulation half-life of nanoparticles [6,9]. For instance, poly(ethylene glycol) (PEG) [27] and zwitterionic carboxybetaine polymers [9] have been demonstrated to greatly increase nanoparticle stability and in vivo pharmacokinetics. In our previous work, pOrnAA brushes were shown to be ultra-low fouling with adsorption of only 1.8ng/cm2 from human serum and 3.2ng/cm2 from human plasma [13]. Thus it is expected that nanogels made from pOrnAA would show similar nonspecific adsorption resistance. The stability of p(CD-OrnAA) and FA-p(CD-OrnAA) nanogels was tested by monitoring their size change in 5 wt% BSA or 5wt% Fg solution in PBS via dynamic light scattering as a function of time. Fig. 2 a and b revealed that both nanogels, before and after FA conjugation, retained their original sizes without aggregation after 24h incubation. The surface conjugation of FA did not significantly affect the nanogel stability, since small amount of the attached ligand did not significantly change their zwitterionic nature.

Hydrogels have been extensively explored as the controlled release drug delivery vehicles due to their excellent biocompatibility, flexible chemical structure design, and adjustable releasing property [28]. Biomacromolecules can be released from the porous gel matrix through diffusion or environmental stimuli such as changes in pH or temperature. In this study, FITC-labeled dextran was used as a model drug to study the nanogel releasing properties. Dextran is a simple model to represent macromolecular hydrophilic drugs (e.g., proteins, peptides, RNA) [29]. FITC-dextran 10K was encapsulated in nanogels during the inverse emulsion polymerization, and the releasing behavior was then monitored as a function of time. As shown in Fig. 3 , around 30% of the initially encapsulated drug was released in 20days, suggesting the diffusion-controlled releasing manner. Since the diffusion profile is affected by gel pore size and drug molecular weight, the releasing speed can be further adjusted by changing the crosslinking density of nanogels and intrinsic properties of drugs. Notice that the thiocarbamoyl linkage between FITC and dextran is stable at room temperature under pH 4–9 for at least one month [30], and the fluorescence intensity increases linearly with the concentration of FITC-dextran in the experimental range (i.e., no self-quenching; see Fig. S6). Therefore, the release profile of FITC-dextran can be evaluated by measuring the fluorescence intensity of the released FITC-dextran.

The cytotoxicity of FA-modified nanogels, unmodified p(CD-OrnAA) nanogels, and CCDs was assessed by measuring the viability of NIH/3T3 fibroblasts. As seen in Fig. 4 , all samples maintained more than 80% viability even at a particle concentration as high as 2.5mg/ml. The viability of cells treated with CCDs was higher than 85%, which is consistent with the literature value [22]. Statistical analysis revealed that the viability of cells treated with p(CD-OrnAA) or FA-p(CD-OrnAA) nanogels at the same concentration was not significantly different (p>0.05). Indeed, 2.5mg/ml is already a pretty high particle concentration since many studies tested the cytotoxicity of drug delivery vehicles at μg/ml levels [31,32]. A zwitterionic poly(carboxybetaine methacrylate) nanogel of ∼100nm in diameter led to ∼70% cell viability at 0.5–2mg/ml [33]. In summary, FA-modified and unmodified p(CD-OrnAA) nanogels both exhibited good biocompatibility.

Non-fouling surface could dramatically increase stability of nanoparticles and extend their in vivo half-life, but it is not without its own set of complications. A complete non-fouling surface could render nanoparticles “stealth” from surroundings or even their targets, thus limits their applications for intracellular drug delivery. Two approaches are generally used to enhance the nanocarrier cell uptake: environmental stimuli-triggered charge switching [34] and surface ligand conjugation [35,36]. The former approach needs complicated chemical structure design; while the latter is not applicable for many non-fouling materials. For example, PEG, poly(sulfobetaine methacrylate) and poly(2-methacryloyloxyethyl phosphorylcholine) do not have functional groups for further conjugation. One of the major advantages of pAAZ polymers, including pOrnAA, is that they can be easily functionalized through either primary amine or carboxyl groups while the remaining background is still non-fouling. In this work, folic acid was conjugated onto the nanogel surfaces as it is a well-established cancer cell targeting ligand [37]. The combination of a cancer cell targeting ligand and non-fouling surfaces would enable the nanocarriers to recognize their destinations. SKOV3 ovarian carcinoma cells were chosen as the model cancer cells to study the cell uptake of nanogels. Fig. 5 shows the confocal microscopic images of cells after 12h incubation with p(CD-OrnAA) and FA-p(CD-OrnAA). As proposed, fluorescence from CCDs was used to track the nanogels. Cell nuclei were stained to be blue under 405-nm excitation. Considering the limited available light source wavelengths (405, 488, and 543nm) of our confocal microscope and to distinguish the nanoparticles from nuclei staining, excitation at 488nm, rather than at a shorter wavelength, was chosen to observe the green nanoparticles. It can be seen that FA-p(CD-OrnAA) was internalized into the cytoplasm of SKOV3 cells, whereas no green fluorescence was detected from cells treated with p(CD-OrnAA). A series of confocal Z-stack images, with the step size of 1.48μm, of SKOV3 cells treated with FA-p(CD-OrnAA) were shown in Fig. 6 . The image taken at z=4.44μm was enlarged and shown in Fig. 7 . The images clearly showed that most FA-p(CD-OrnAA) nanogels were distributed inside the cytoplasm of cells, rather than on cell surfaces. To confirm the cell uptake was facilitated by folate receptor mediated internalization, a competition assay was performed by pretreating SKOV3 cells with abundant FA before the introduction of FA-p(CD-OrnAA). As shown in Fig. 5 (right column), no nanogel fluorescence was observed inside the cells when folate receptors were pre-saturated, indicating that the internalization of FA-p(CD-OrnAA) was fulfilled via specific interactions between FA and its receptor.

As it is vitally important for drug delivery nanoparticles to distinguish cancer cells from normal tissue cells to reduce adverse effects during therapy, the nanogel targeting selectivity was further tested using a normal tissue cell line. NIH/3T3 fibroblasts were examined after incubating with p(CD-OrnAA) or FA-(CD-OrnAA) for 12h. As shown in Fig. 8 , no detectable green fluorescence was observed from p(CD-OrnAA) or FA-p(CD-OrnAA) treated NIH/3T3 cells, indicating the folate receptor deficient normal cells cannot internalize these nanoparticles. The in vitro cellular uptake experiments in both folate receptor overexpressed and deficient cell lines prove that nanogels composed of non-fouling materials with targeting ligands can effectively deliver their cargoes into selected cells.

In summary, a multifunctional nanogel drug delivery system was designed by copolymerization of zwitterionic amino acid-derived antifouling monomer OrnAA with fluorescent crosslinkable carbon dots. The non-fouling pOrnAA endued the nanogels with great stability in biophysical environments, whereas the abundant functional groups can be further conjugated with biomolecules. Fluorescent CCD crosslinkers provided stable fluorescence signals for real-time tracking in the delivery process. A model drug dextran loaded in nanogels was shown to be released in a controlled manner. Folic acid-modified nanogels were shown to be only selectively internalized in folate receptor overexpressed cancer cells. Such multifunctional zwitterionic nanogels hold great potential as targeted drug delivery vectors because of their great stability, bioimaging capability, excellent biocompatibility, controlled drug release, and selective cell targeting.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to thank Dr. Gang Cheng for the use of DLS instrument, Dr. Jie Zheng for the access to the fluorescence spectrophotometer, Dr. Jiahua Zhu for the use of UV–vis spectrophotometer, and Mr. Tuo Ji for his help with the TEM characterization. This work is supported by the University of Akron faculty start-up fund.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.actbio.2016.04.006.
